coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent "turn-on" aptamer sensor for sensitive and reliable detection of Golgi glycoprotein 73 based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and molybdenum disulfide nanosheets
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Xinjun
Fang, Fengyan
Na, Jintong
Yan, Runjie
Huang, Yong
Zhou, Zhide
Zhao, Yongxiang
Li, Guiyin
coredata.dc:description: The sensitivity and specificity of Golgi glycoprotein 73 (GP73) are very important for early diagnosis of hepatocellular carcinoma. Herein, we constructed a new-fashioned fluorescent aptamer sensor for GP73 determination based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDS) and molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) nanosheets. N-GQDs with high fluorescence intensity and good stability were screened out, and GP73 aptamer (GP73Apt) is labeled with N-GQDs to form the N-GQDs-GP73Apt fluorescence probe. MoS2 nanosheets can quench the fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt owing to fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanisms. After introducing GP73 into the biosensing system, the N-GQDs-GP73Apt specifically bound with GP73 to form the deployable structures, making N-GQDs-GP73Apt far away from MoS2, blocking the fluorescence energy transfer process, and restoring the fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt. When the GP73 concentration was in the extent of 2.5 ng/mL∼100 ng/mL, the relative fluorescence recovery is linearly relevant to the concentration of GP73, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.29 ng/mL (S/N = 3). Moreover in the application of actual serum sample detection, the recovery was range 98.85∼100.55 %. The fluorescent aptamer sensor can rapidly detect and analyze the serum marker GP73 with the characteristics of low-cost, high sensitivity, good specificity and recovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Molybdenum disulfide
Golgi glycoprotein 73
Fluorescent aptamer sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure of detection GP73 with the fluorescence aptasensor
Analysis of GP73 in actual human serum samples
Principle of the fluorescent aptasensor based on N-GQDs and MoS2 nanosheets
Fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs-GP73Apt by MoS2
Time dependence of the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system
Detection of GP73 in standard aqueous solution
Specificity and selectivity of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 fluorescent aptamer sensor
Determination of GP73 in actual serum samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Open Fund of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Bio-targeting Theranostics
State Project for Essential Drug Research and Development

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Projects of Talents Recruitment of GDUPT ( RC-XJ2022000401 ), the Open Fund of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Bio-targeting Theranostics (No. GXSWBX201903 ), the State Project for Essential Drug Research and Development (No. 2019ZX09301132 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of N-GQDs; (B) Fluorescence excitation spectrum emission spectrum and emission spectrum of N-GQDs (the inset is the picture for N-GQDs under natural and ultraviolet light); (C) TEM image of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2; (D) UV–vis spectra of MoS2.

(A) TEM characterization of N-GQDs-GP73Apt; (B) The UV-Vis spectra of GP73Apt, N-GQDs and N-GQDs-GP73Apt; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs, N-GQDs-GP73Apt, N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 and N-GQDs-GP73Apt-GP73-MoS2; (D) Fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs-GP73Apt and UV spectra of MoS2.

(A) N-GQDs-GP73Apt fluorescence quenched by different concentrations of MoS2; (B) Time dependence of fluorescence quenching degree of N-GQDs-GP73Apt induced by MoS2; (C) The particle sizes changes of the different process for the constructed N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2-GP73 system; (D) The Zeta potential changes of the different process for the constructed N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2-GP73 system.
Condition optimization of (A) Incubation temperature, (B) pH of PBS buffer, (C) Incubation time and (D) Concentration of N-GQDs -GP73Apt.

(A) and (B) The fluorescence recovery of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 to different concentrations of GP73 from 2.5 ng/mL to 1200 ng/mL; (C) Calibration plot of the relative fluorescence recovery [(F1-F0)/F0] against the GP73 concentration; (D) Selectivity for possible interfering substances of the proposed fluorescent aptasensor.

(A) The principle of GP73 detection based on N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system conjunction with specific binding of GP73 aptamer to GP73; (B) The feasibility of the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system to detect GP73.
Detection of GP73 in clinical serum sample using the established fluorescence strategy (n = 3).
Sample recoveries and coefficients of various methods for GP73 determination.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent "turn-on" aptamer sensor for sensitive and reliable detection of Golgi glycoprotein 73 based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and molybdenum disulfide nanosheets

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The sensitivity and specificity of Golgi glycoprotein 73 (GP73) are very important for early diagnosis of hepatocellular carcinoma. Herein, we constructed a new-fashioned fluorescent aptamer sensor for GP73 determination based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDS) and molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) nanosheets. N-GQDs with high fluorescence intensity and good stability were screened out, and GP73 aptamer (GP73Apt) is labeled with N-GQDs to form the N-GQDs-GP73Apt fluorescence probe. MoS2 nanosheets can quench the fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt owing to fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanisms. After introducing GP73 into the biosensing system, the N-GQDs-GP73Apt specifically bound with GP73 to form the deployable structures, making N-GQDs-GP73Apt far away from MoS2, blocking the fluorescence energy transfer process, and restoring the fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt. When the GP73 concentration was in the extent of 2.5 ng/mL∼100 ng/mL, the relative fluorescence recovery is linearly relevant to the concentration of GP73, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.29 ng/mL (S/N = 3). Moreover in the application of actual serum sample detection, the recovery was range 98.85∼100.55 %. The fluorescent aptamer sensor can rapidly detect and analyze the serum marker GP73 with the characteristics of low-cost, high sensitivity, good specificity and recovery.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent "turn-on" aptamer sensor was designed for sensitive and reliable detection of Golgi glycoprotein 73 based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and molybdenum disulfide nanosheets. The GP73 biosensor responses linearly from 2.5 to 100 ng/mL with LOD of 1.29 ng/mL (S/N = 3), and shows acceptable specificity and good recovery.

ce:section.ce:para: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is a primary malignant tumor of liver cells, which is the most common form of liver cancer in life. It accounts for a large proportion of cancer deaths worldwide. Generally, HCC patients can be diagnosed only after deterioration, and the survival time after diagnosis is expressed in months. The number of new cases of every year accounts for about 55 % of the world's total and the 5-year overall survival rate is about 10 %. Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) was an early specific marker for the HCC diagnosis. However, the sensitivity and specificity of AFP for HCC were limited [1]. Golgi transmembrane glycoprotein 73 (GP73) was first discovered in 2000 by Kladney RD and subsequent studies have shown that GP73 is an ideal serum markers for inchoate diagnosis and evaluation of postoperative recurrence of HCC. The sensitivity and specificity of GP73 are much higher than alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) [2]. Ning AI et al. [3] found that the expression level of GP73 in the serum of patients with HCC was significantly higher than that of normal people. With the further deepening of research, it was found that the expression level of GP73 is different in different stages of liver disease [4]. Therefore, the study of GP73 as a marker for auxiliary diagnosis and postoperative observation of liver cancer plays a pivotal role in the diagnosis and treatment of HCC.

At present, the methods commonly used to detect GP73 protein included western blot, enzyme-linked immunesorbent assays (ELISA), electrochemical assays and immunofluorescence [5]. The first two methods are conventional methods for the detection of GP73 with high sensitivity, while these methods require harsh reaction conditions, complicated operations and limit their further application in the clinic. The electrochemical assay is sensitive but unstable. The fluorescent assay offers easy operation, fast analysis, low cost and high sensitivity, making it an effective method for the detection of a wide range of tumor markers [6]. Particularly, fluorescent aptamer sensors have been developed to detect cancer markers in recent years based on the change of fluorescence signal before and after the interaction between aptamer and target molecules [7]. Currently, there are four main sensing mechanisms for fluorescent aptamer sensors based on nanoparticles and aptamers, including fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET), internal filter effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and aggregation induced quenching (ACQ) [8]. Among them, FRET has been widely applied as the important sensing mode based on the fluorescent quenching or enhancement mechanism. FRET is a phenomenon that the emission spectrum of the fluorescent donor molecule overlaps the absorption spectrum of the receptor quenched fluorescent molecule. When the distance between the two molecules is less than 10 nm, energy transfer occurs between the fluorescent donor and the fluorescent receptor. Thus, the fluorescence intensity of the donor is far lower than the fluorescence intensity when it exists alone, that is, fluorescence quenching [9]. The fluorescent aptasensors have wider output range, lower cost, higher specificity as well as better anti-interference than other types of immunosensors. Li G et al. [10] designed a highly sensitive fluorescent aptasensor for glypican-3 (GPC3, another HCC biomarker) detection stemmed from the principle of FRET, in which GPC3 aptamer (GPC3Apt)-labeled gold carbon dots (AuCDs-GPC3Apt) were served as the fluorescent donor and magnetic graphene oxide (Fe3O4/GO) nanosheets were used as the fluorescent acceptor. In addition, the FRET quenching mechanism has been applied in the development of biosensors through integration with nanostructured materials. Various nanomaterials such as gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [11], graphene oxide (GO) [12], molybdenum disulfide sheet (MoS2) [13] and palladium nanosheets [14] have been designed as fluorescence quenchers. Among them, MoS2 nanosheet is one of the most commonly fluorescent quencher because of its wide absorption band from 200 to 800 nm and good electron mobility, and high electron density. For example, Peng X et al. [15] designed a simple and portable nanofiber paper (NFP)-based fluorescence “off-on” immunosensor for programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1) rapid determination with the detection limit of 85.5 pg/mL, in which MoS2 nanosheet was used as fluorescent quencher and CdSe/ZnS quantum dots as FRET donor. So, it is reasonable and innovative to develop a MoS2-based platform for GP73 detection with expecting an advantageous and excellent fluorescence performance.

On the other hand, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a sort of quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterials with stable fluorescence light source [16,17]. GQDs have good biocompatibility and water solubility, wide excitation spectrum, strong quantum confinement and edge effect. Compared with semiconductor quantum dots (QD) and organic dyes, GQDs have the disadvantages of low quantum yield and short fluorescence lifetime, which limit their practical biosensor applications [18]. It has been reported that heteroatom doping or surface functionalization can ameliorate the electronic and optical properties of GQDs [19]. Nitrogen doping can change the density of electron clouds on the surface of GQDs and regulate their physicochemical properties, making them highly fluorescent stable and less prone to bleaching, and therefore also less prone to serious fluorescence decay [20–22]. For instance, Kalkal a et al. [22] designed a fluorescent biosensor to detect neuron specific enolase (NSE) using N-GQDs as energy donor and AuNPs as energy receptor. The fluorescence NSE biosensor exhibited fast response time (16 min), broader linear detection range (0.1 pg/mL to 1000 ng/mL), and remarkably low detection limit (0.09 pg/mL).

Aptamer is an artificially designed single-stranded oligonucleotide (ssDNA or RNA) through systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment, SELEX) technology [23]. Aptamers have a series of advantages that other natural antibodies cannot match, such as simple preparation and storage, direct in vitro screening, strong target specificity, and high biological affinity. Furthermore, aptamers are able to be easily and directly attached to nanodrugs, sensors, signal enhancers, or other functionalized nanobiocarrier materials. Due to the advantages of aptamer, it is gradually replacing the traditional antibody molecules to be widely used in clinical research [24]. One ssDNA aptamer (A10–2) that can specifically bind HCC marker protein GP73 was screened from a random oligonucleotide library by SELEX technique. The identified aptamer A10–2 could specifically bind GP73 with high binding capacity (K
d =127.4 ± 18.65 nM) [25]. Our group [26] have designed a sandwich electrochemical sensor for the determination of GP73 by using the GP73 aptamer as identified element and hemin-reduced graphene oxide-manganese oxide (H-rGO-Mn3O4) nanozyme as signal probe. Another study [27] has been constructed a colorimetric GP73 biosensor with the limit of detection (LOD) of 4.7 ng/mL based on GP73 aptamer and reduced graphene oxide-carboxymethyl chitosan-Hemin/platinum@palladium nanozyme.

In this study, the aptamer of GP73 with high sensitivity and specificity, N-GQDs with stable fluorescent light source and molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) with high quenching efficiency were selected to develop a fluorescence aptamer sensor for detection GP73. Aminosylated GP73 aptamer was combined with N-GQDs to form fluorescence N-GQDs-GP73Apt probe. Due to the van der Waals force and hydrogen bonding effect of MoS2 to aptamer molecules, the nucleic acid bases in single-stranded nucleic acid (ssDNA) can be physically adsorbed on the sheet of MoS2 [28], thus quenching the fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt thanks to FRET mechanism. When GP73 is added into the fluorescence biosensing platform, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs-GP73Apt is restored as the binding affinity between GP73 and the aptamer is stronger than the interaction between the aptamer and MoS2. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 under different experimental conditions and the sensitivity of detecting GP73 in serum were discussed, and the detection mechanism of GP73 was clarified.

GP73 protein and aminated GP73 aptamer (GP73Apt) were purchased from Sangon Bioengineering Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tris-hydrochloride (Tris-HCI) was purchased from Shanghai Qiaoxing Trading Co., Ltd. Ammonia and sodium hydroxide 96 % (NaOH) were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Potassium chloride (KCl) and magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·H2O) were purchased from Xilong Science Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Carbodiimide (EDAC) was purchased from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O) was purchased from Xinjian Fine Chemical Plant (Guangzhou, China). HEPES buffer was purchased from Soleibo Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS), Molybdenum Dioxide (MoS2), Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO), Serum Alpha Fetoprotein (AFP), Human Serum Albumin (HAS), Immunoglobulin G (IgG) and Immunoglobulin Protein E (IgE) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All chemicals and reagents used were of analytical grade and prepared with deionized water.

In this study, N-GQDs were prepared using citric acid as carbon source and ammonia water as nitrogen source. Take 2.0 g of citric acid (CA) and 0.3 mL of ammonia in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 210 ℃ for 6 h. The resulting dark brown mixed solution was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. Then, the N-GQDs dispersion was dropped with a small amount of 1 mol/L NaOH aqueous solution until the pH was adjusted to 7.0. To remove impurities, the pH-adjusted N-GQDs dispersion was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was obtained by centrifugation and diluted to 200 mL with ultrapure water to obtain an N-GQDs solution with a concentration of 10 mg/mL and a bright yellow color. Store the prepared N-GQDs solution in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

First, the prepared N-GQDs were diluted to a concentration of 8 μg/mL with PBS solution (pH =7.0). Then, 30 μL of EDAC solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL was added to 100 μL of 8 μg/mL N-GQDs solution. Activated by ultrasonic vibration at 25 °C. Take 1.5 μL of 100 μM amino-modified GP73 aptamer, and then add 98.5 μL of ultrapure water for aptamers, and the concentration of the obtained aptamer is 1.5 μM. Mix 100 μL of activated N-GQDs solution with 100 μL of GP73 aptamer by shaking for 2 min, and then incubate the mixture in a shaking incubator at 25 °C for 1 h. After the incubation, the N-GQDs-GP73Apt compound was obtained.

In this experiment, MoS2 was used as the fluorescence quencher of the FRET system. Taking 20 μL of MoS2 suspension with a concentration of 5 mg/mL, and then adding 80 μL of DMSO to dilute the MoS2 suspension with a concentration of 1.0 mg/mL. 200 μL of N-GQDs-GP73Apt solution was added to 100 μL of MoS2 suspension, shaken evenly, and then placed in a shaking incubator at 25 °C for 1 h. After incubation, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 was measured (denoted as F0) under λex/em of 310/400 nm and compared with that of N-GQDs-GP73Apt and N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure of detection GP73 with the fluorescence aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: 50 μL of GP73 solutions with various concentration were added to 150 μL of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 solution, and incubated in a 25 °C incubator for 80 min. F1 represents the restored fluorescence degree for different concentrations of GP73 (λex/em=310/400 nm) were record under λex/em of 310/400 nm (denoted as F1). Recovery rate of fluorescence was calculated using the formula (F1-F0)/F0. The known concentration of GP73 and the corresponding fluorescence recovery were plotted and the regression linear equation was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of GP73 in actual human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The utility of the fluorescent aptasensor was studied in actual human serum samples obtained by the recovery experiments. Normal human serum samples were obtained from the Guangxi Key Laboratory of Metabolic Disease Research, 924 Hospital of the Chinese People's Liberation Army (Guilin, China). First, 2.0 μL of GP73 standard solutions of known various concentrations (20, 40, 120 ng/mL) were mixed with 2.0 μL of normal human serum as spiked samples. Then, the spiked sample (4.0 μL) was mixed with the prepared N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 fluorescence sensor (12.0 μL) and incubated at 25 °C for 80 min, Lastly, the fluorescence emission was recorded (λex/em=310/400 nm) three times in parallel measurement. The concentration of GP73 in spiked sample was determined by the calculated recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the fluorescent aptasensor based on N-GQDs and MoS2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: In this research, stemmed from the targeted binding of GP73 aptamer (GP73Apt) to GP73 and MoS2 as a quencher for N-GQDs, the N-GQDs-MoS2-based fluorescent aptamer sensor was constructed, as shown in Scheme 1A. The detection process of the aptamer sensor consisted of two stages: quenching and recovering fluorescence. GP73Apt modified N-GQDs (N-GQDs-GP73Apt) with good stability and high fluorescence efficiency were prepared by the coupling reaction of ultrasonic oscillation in EADC solution. N-GQDs-GP73Apt is able to absorb onto the MoS2 nanosheets via Van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding effect between GP73Apt and MoS2, which will lead to fluorescence resonance energy transfer between N-GQDs-GP73Apt and MoS2 nanosheets, thus quench the fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt. When GP73 protein is added to the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system, GP73 first specifically recognizes its specific aptamer and bind to N-GQDs-GP73Apt, causing the secondary structure of GP73Apt change and forming the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-GP73 complexes. Then, the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-GP73 complexes is rapidly exfoliated from the MoS2 surface, blocking the process of fluorescence energy transfer and resulting in recovery of fluorescence. Depending on the extent of recovery of fluorescence, high-sensitivity quantitative determination of GP73 is achieved.

Furthermore, we investigated whether the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system was feasible to detect GP73, the fluorescence quenching and recovery experiments were investigated and the results were shown in Scheme 1B. N-GQDs-GP73Apt had a maximum intensity of fluorescence (2510) at λex/em of 310/400 nm (Scheme 1B, curve a). In the absence of GP73 (that is the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system) (Scheme 1B, curve b), the intensity of fluorescence decreased to 818.4, suggesting that MoS2 had an intense quenching effect on the fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt. When the GP73 was present in the system, the fluorescence intensity increased conspicuously (2058, GP73 concentration = 1.0 ng/mL (Scheme 1B, curve c) and the fluorescence intensity restored to 2408 with 2.0 ng/mL GP73 (Scheme 1B, curve d), which might be explained by the specific recognition and binding of N-GQDs-GP73Apt and GP73, resulting in the changes of secondary structure of the GP73Apt and forming the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-GP73 complexes. On this condition, the interaction between N-GQDs-GP73Apt and MoS2 is weakened and N-GQDs-GP73Apt-GP73 complexes are far away from the surface of MoS2, bringing about the recovery of fluorescence. With the increase of GP73 concentration, the degree of fluorescence recovery gradually increased. Thus, the designed fluorescent sensor exerted well responses to GP73.

Herein, the fluorescent aptamer sensor was constructed using MoS2 as the fluorescent acceptor and N-GQDs-GP73Apt as the donor. Upon binding of the N-GQDs-GP73Apt to the MoS2, N-GQDs-GP73Apt is absorbed onto the MoS2 nanosheets via van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding effect [28], part or all of the energy is transferred from the donor to the acceptor, thus turning off the fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system. But when GP73 is introduced into the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system, N-GQDs-GP73Apt tends to combine with GP73 to form the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-GP73 complex. On this condition, the interaction between N-GQDs-GP73Apt and MoS2 is weakened and N-GQDs-GP73Apt-GP73 complexes are far away from the surface of MoS2 nanosheets, making the increase of the distance between the fluorescent donor N-GQDs and acceptor MoS2, blocking the energy transfer processes and restoring the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs-GP73Apt. The intensity of fluorescence could be restored to varying extent by the addition of difference GP73 in the system. Thus, the aptamer sensor displays positive analyte-dependent fluorescence intensity for determination target GP73.

Fig. 1 A shows a typical TEM image of N-GQDs, the dark black dots can be seen and the size of the N-GQDs is much smaller than 10 nm. Fig. 1B shows the fluorescence excitation spectra (the blue line) and emission spectra of N-GQDs (the red line). N-GQDs had a maximum excitation peak under 310 nm and a maximum emission peak under 400 nm emission light. N-GQDs exhibited obvious blue fluorescence when 310 nm was used as the excitation light, yellowish in natural light (inset in Fig. 1B), which indicated that N-GQDs possessed stable blue fluorescence property, contributing to the improvement of the sensitivity and stability of the biosensor.

Fig. 1 C is the TEM image of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2, which shows that the black film is MoS2, and the smaller dot-like particles on the film are N-GQDs-GP73Apt. We can see that N-GQDs-GP73Apt has been bound to MoS2, indicating that the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 FRET system is constructed success.
Fig. 1D is the UV absorption spectrum of MoS2 nanosheets which is strong in the UV region and weak in the visible spectrum. The MoS2 nanosheets have an absorption maximum at 240 nm.

The fluorescent probe N-GQDs-GP73Apt is formed by EDAC coupled action between the carboxyl group of N-GQDs and the amino dehydration of GP73Apt. Fig. 2 A shows the TEM image of N-GQDs-GP73Apt, a large number of particles are uniformly distributed in the figure, and the diameters are less than 100 nm. Fig. 2B is the UV-Vis spectra of GP73Apt, N-GQDs and N-GQDs-GP73Apt. The single GP73Apt has an absorption maximum at 260 nm (Fig. 2B, curve a), while the absorption maximum peaks of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-GP73Apt are both at 220 nm (Fig. 2B, curves b and c). The absorption peak of N-GQDs-GP73Apt at 260 nm is low, which is due to the adsorption of GP73Apt on the surface of N-GQDs. Fig. 2 C shows the fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs, N-GQDs-GP73Apt, N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 and N-GQDs-GP73Apt- GP73-MoS2. All these solutions have the same shape fluorescence emission spectra and the maximum emission wavelength is at 400 nm. The fluorescence intensity at 400 nm for N-GQDs was the maximum and N-GQDs-GP73Apt had less fluorescence intensity than that of N-GQDs. When N-GQDs-GP73Apt combined with MoS2, the peak of fluorescence intensity was the lowest because of FRET effect. When GP73 was added, GP73Apt was specifically recognized and combined with GP73, blocking the energy transfer processes, so that the fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-GP73 recovered. The results in Fig. 2 C show that MoS2 binds to N-GQDs-GP73Apt and plays a quenching effect.

Fig. 2D shows the fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs-GP73Apt (red line) and UV spectra of MoS2 (blue line). The absorption spectrum of MoS2 nanosheets (Fig. 2D, blue line) overlaps with the excitation spectrum of fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt when the spectrum is from 325 nm to 500 nm (Fig. 2D, red line), which indicates that the FRET appearance can occur between MoS2 and N-GQDs-GP73Apt [15]. When the emission spectrum of fluorescence donor N-GQDs-GP73Apt has some overlap with the absorption spectrum of acceptor MoS2, and the distance between energy donor and energy acceptor is suitable, the fluorescence energy is transferred from donor to acceptor, thus MoS2 quenches the fluorescence of fluorescence donor N-GQDs-GP73Apt.

The stability of N-GQDs-GP73Apt was investigated in different pH buffer solutions and in different times in a UV irradiated environment, respectively. The N-GQDs-GP73Apt has stable fluorescence intensity in the pH range of 6.0–8.5 (Fig. S1), excluding the influence of different pH environments on the stability of N-GQDs-GP73Apt. Also, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs-GP73Apt is stable under UV light irradiation for 120 min (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs-GP73Apt by MoS2
ce:section.ce:para: As we know, the fluorescence intensity is largely affected by the ratio of quencher molecules to fluorescence probes. Therefore, in order to obtain the highest quenching rate of the fluorescent aptamer sensor, the concentration of the quencher MoS2 nano sheet was optimized. In short, the concentrations of MoS2 were set to 0 ng/mL, 50 ng/mL, 100 ng/mL, 200 ng/mL, 500 ng/mL, 800 ng/mL, 1000 ng/mL and 2000 ng/mL. 100 μL MoS2 with different concentrations was added into 200 μL N-GQDs-GP73Apt solution, and then oscillated uniformly. The fluorescence intensity (λex/em=310/400 nm) was measured after 20 min and the result was shown in Fig. 3 A. Seen from Fig. 3 A, N-GQDs-GP73Apt has the greatest fluorescence intensity in the absence of MoS2. With the increase of MoS2 concentration from 0 μg/mL to 1000 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually, that is MoS2 quenches the fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt gradually. When the concentration of MoS2 is from 1000 μg/mL to 2000 μg/mL, the fluorescence spectra are very close, which shows that increasing the concentration of MoS2 cannot enhance the quenching phenomenon, the quenching effect of MoS2 on N-GQDs-GP73Apt is close to the zero point of agglomeration. The optimum concentration of MoS2 is 1000 μg/mL when the quenching efficiency is the highest. So MoS2 concentration of 1000 μg/mL is selected for further experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time dependence of the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3B shows the time dependence of fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 FRET system. As can be seen from Fig. 3B that the intensity of fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system decreased with the increase of reaction time from 0 to 20 min, which indicates that MoS2 and N-GQDs-GP73Apt are constantly reacting during 20 min. With the gradual progress of the reaction, the phenomenon of fluorescent energy transfer between the fluorescent donor and the receptor is gradually enhanced. After 20 min, the fluctuation of fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 tended to be flat, indicating that the reaction between MoS2 and N-GQDs-GP73Apt is basically completed and the fluorescence quenching effect reaches the best state. Therefore, the incubation time of MoS2 conjugated with N-GQDs-GP73Apt was 20 min to ensure the highest fluorescence quenching efficiency.

Moreover the changes of particle size and surface potential of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2-GP73 system were studied by dynamic light scattering and Zeta potential measurement (Fig. 3 C and 3D). The sizes of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-GP73Apt were 388.2 ± 46.9 nm and 708.8 ± 80.9 nm, respectively. After adding MoS2, the particle size of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 decreased to 579.4 ± 41.5 nm since the strong absorption of GP73Apt on the surface MoS2. N-GQDs-GP73Apt is closely adsorbed on the surface of since MoS2 through van der Waals force and low surface negative charge. Consequently, the particle size of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 is smaller than that of N-GQDs-GP73Apt. When GP73 protein exists in the solution, the particle size of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-GP73-MoS2 increases significantly (2689 ± 475.5 nm), which is due to the combination of GP73 with N-GQDs-GP73Apt to form a complex structure of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-GP73-MoS2, and N-GQDs-GP73Apt-GP73 is released from the MoS2 surface.

Additionally, the Zeta potentials of MoS2 and N-GQDs were − 21.8 mV and 8.23 ± −15.1 mV, respectively. The Zeta potential of N-GQDs-GP73Apt slightly enlarged (−3.57 mV), which may be due to the coupling reaction between carboxyl group of N-GQDs and amino group of GP73Apt induced by EDAC couple linking. After MoS2 and N-GQDs-GP73Apt were mixed, the Zeta potential continued to increase (−1.54 mV) due to adsorption effect. Subsequently, when GP73 was added to the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 sytem, the Zeta potential decreased to − 22.9 mV, which was caused by the binding affinity between GP73 and its corresponding GP73Apt, which induced the change of complex on MoS2 surface.

Some factors affecting fluorescence recovery were optimized by experiments in order to allow the fluorescent biosensor to perform the best detection function. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 was expressed by F0. F1 represented the fluorescence intensity after GP73 addition into the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system. The relative fluorescence recovery is expressed as (F1-F0)/F0.

First of all, the effects of different reaction temperatures on the fluorescence recovery of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2-GP73 system were studied ( Fig. 4A). The relative fluorescence recovery of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-GP73-MoS2 system gradually increased in the range of 4–25 °C. When the temperature is gradually within the scope of 25–35 ℃, the relative fluorescence recovery of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-GP73-MoS2 system gradually decreased. Consequently, 25 ℃ is the best reaction temperature for the recovery of fluorescence.

The effect of pH values of different PBS solution on the relative fluorescence recovery is shown in Fig. 4B. When pH value is 7.0, the relative fluorescence recovery is the highest. This indicates that neutral environment has the least effect on the recovery of fluorescence intensity in N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system. Therefore, the optimum pH value for the reaction between N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 and GP73 is 7.0.

The effect of reaction time between GP73 and N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 systems on the relative fluorescence recovery was studied (Fig. 4C). When the reaction time is 0–80 min, the recovery rate of fluorescence gradually increases with the increase of time. After 80 min, the fluctuation of relative fluorescence recovery tends to be flat, which indicates that the reaction between GP73 and N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 reaches saturation after 80 min. Therefore, 80 min is the best reaction time for the recovery experiment of fluorescence.

Various concentrations of N-GQDs-GP73Apt added to N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system produced different fluorescence recoveries. Results as shown in Fig. 4D, the relative fluorescence recovery increased with the increase of N-GQDs-GP73Apt concentration, and the curve gradually tended to be flat after the N-GQDs-GP73Apt concentration reached 2.5 ng/mL, indicating that the concentration of N-GQDs-GP73Apt was positively correlated with the recovery of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the recovery of fluorescence experiment was carried out at the N-GQDs-GP73Apt concentration of 2.5 ng/mL.

The optimum reaction conditions of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 and GP73 were obtained: reaction temperature: 25 ℃, pH of PBS buffer: 7.0, reaction time: 80 min, and the concentration of N-GQDs-GP73Apt: 2.5 ng/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GP73 in standard aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum reaction conditions, the dependence of GP73 concentration on fluorescence intensity was further verified. As shown in Fig. 5 A and 5B, different concentrations of GP73 (from 2.5 ng/mL to 2500 ng/mL) were added to N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system, and the intensity of fluorescence recovered, due to the detachment of N-GQDs from MoS2 nanosheets which was caused by the conformational change of the GP73Apt binding with GP73. With the increase of GP73 protein concentration, the relative fluorescence recovery (F1-F0)/F0 also increased. There was a linear dependence between dependent the relative fluorescence recovery and GP73 concentration ranging from 2.5 ng/mL to 100 ng/mL (Fig. 5 C). The linear regression equation is Y= 0.00146X + 2.67119E-4 (where Y represents the relative fluorescence recovery and X represents GP73 protein concentration), and the related coefficient is R2 = 0.99654. The limit of detection (LOD) of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 fluorescence resonance GP73 aptamer sensor is 1.29 ng/mL (S/N = 3). Compared with antibodies, aptamers, as targets, have the characteristics of good stability, easy acquisition, moderate price and small immune response [33]. Therefore, when the concentration of GP73 is in the range of 2.5 ng/mL∼100 ng/mL, the fluorescence sensor has good stability and easy to obtain, and its manufacturing cost is low and the detection time is short, which is expected to solve the early clinical diagnosis of liver cancer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and selectivity of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 fluorescent aptamer sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the good performance of the constructed fluorescence sensor, the interfering substances with the same concentration as GP73 protein (such as AFP, IgG, IgE, HSA) were mixed with N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 solution for fluorescence recovery experiment, and the selectivity of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 sensor was tested under the same experimental steps and conditions. Fig. 5D shows the fluorescence intensity recovered by GP73 protein to N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system is much higher than that of other proteins, and N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 can accurately react with GP73 protein. The recovery rate of fluorescence obtained by the mixture is close to that obtained by adding GP73 protein alone. Therefore, it can be inferred that N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 fluorescence sensor has specificity and selectivity for GP73 protein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GP73 in actual serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify that the constructed fluorescence sensor can be applied to actual human serum samples, the N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 fluorescence sensor was prepared under the optimal experimental conditions, and then reacted with the mixture of serum and GP73 protein to carry out fluorescence analysis. The concentration of GP73 in spiked sample was plotted by the linear regression equation is Y= 0.00146X+ 2.67119E-4. And the recovery rate was calculated and listed in Table 1. The lowest recovery rate of fluorescence GP73 sensor is 98.85 %, the highest is 100.55 %, and its relative standard deviation (RSD) is in the range of 1.65∼3.03 %. As shown in Table 2, compared with the determination of other national standards, our determination assay has lower recovery, and the RSD is smaller than that of other methods. These indicate that N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2-based fluorescence assay has a good application prospect in the efficient detection of early hepatocellular carcinoma.

To sum up, we have successfully constructed a fluorescence GP73 sensor based on N-GQDs-GP73Apt and MoS2 nanosheets. MoS2 can quench the fluorescence of N-GQDs-GP73Apt stemmed from FRET mechanism. After adding GP73 into N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 system, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs-GP73Apt is restored as the binding affinity between GP73 and the aptamer is stronger than the interaction between the aptamer and MoS2. The relative fluorescence recovery is linearly related to the concentration of GP73 in the range of 2.5 ng/mL∼100 ng/mL. The limit of detection (LOD) of N-GQDs-GP73Apt-MoS2 fluorescence GP73 aptamer sensor is 1.29 ng/mL (S/N = 3). The fluorescence GP73 aptamer sensor has been successfully applied to the detection GP73 in human serum samples with a recovery of 98.85–100.55 %. The fluorescence sensor can rapidly detect GP73 and has the characteristics of simplicity, portability and sensitivity, which has a good application prospect in biochemical sensors for efficient detection of early hepatocellular carcinoma.
The human serum samples used in this study were approved by the Guangxi Key Laboratory of Metabolic Diseases Research Ethics Committee in Guilin, China.

Xinjun Sun: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Fengyan Fang: Methodology, Visualization, Data curation. Jintong Na: Software, Investigation, Validation. Runjie Yan: Investigation, Data curation. Methodology. Yong Huang: Methodology, Visualization.Yongxiang Zhao: Project administration, Resources, Conceptualization. Zhide Zhou: Project administration, Resources, Conceptualization.Guiyin Li: Project administration, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Projects of Talents Recruitment of GDUPT (RC-XJ2022000401), the Open Fund of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Bio-targeting Theranostics (No. GXSWBX201903), the State Project for Essential Drug Research and Development (No. 2019ZX09301132).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jpba.2022.115215.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
